current_fact.fact_text: On May 17, 2006, the retired aircraft carrier USS Oriskany was deliberately sunk in the Gulf of Mexico to create one of the world’s largest artificial reefs. The 275-meter ‘Mighty O’ transformed from a Cold War warship into a thriving underwater skyscraper for coral, fish, and divers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

current_fact.match_reason: A sunken carrier reborn as a reef mirrors the episode’s water imagery, immersion metaphors, and gentle aquatic flow.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1673, French-Canadian explorer Louis Jolliet and Jesuit missionary Jacques Marquette set off to explore the Mississippi River, seeking a water route that might connect the Great Lakes to the Gulf of Mexico. Guided by Indigenous knowledge, they mapped huge stretches of an inland ‘sea’ that Europeans barely knew existed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #07 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A river expedition and a boat-themed, sea-crossing playlist both trace watery journeys that connect distant shores and cultures.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1527, Pánfilo de Narváez sailed from Spain with about 600 men to explore and conquer Florida; by 1536, only four survivors staggered out of the North American interior. Their eight-year odyssey across what is now the U.S. South and northern Mexico produced some of the earliest European descriptions of the continent’s landscapes and Indigenous nations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A doomed transatlantic expedition and a modern sailing trip both center on life at sea, shared hardship, and how journeys reshape the people onboard.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1809, Napoleon ordered the annexation of the Papal States, effectively folding the Pope’s temporal domain into the French Empire. It was a bold fusion of cannon and cassock, where a secular emperor tried to redraw centuries of spiritual and political geography with a signature.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: An episode steeped in Italian landscapes and identity pairs with the moment a foreign empire tried to redraw Italy’s political and spiritual map.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1012, the powerful counts of Tusculum maneuvered their own kinsman onto the papal throne as Sergius IV, turning a supposedly sacred election into a family project. It’s an early example of how dynasties treated the papacy as another office to inherit, long before modern notions of church-state separation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

queue.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode delve into deep-rooted Italian lineages and how family, place, and power intertwine across centuries.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1648, a combined French and Swedish army defeated Imperial and Bavarian forces at the Battle of Zusmarshausen, one of the last major clashes of the Thirty Years’ War. The battle helped grind down the Holy Roman Empire’s military strength just months before the war’s end reshaped Europe’s religious and political balance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: A brutal 17th‑century battlefield and an episode about women storming rock’s gates both echo themes of confrontation, power, and rewriting who gets to ‘command’ the stage.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1937, the Spanish government led by Francisco Largo Caballero resigned in the wake of the Barcelona May Days, fierce street battles between rival factions on the same Republican side. The crisis ushered in Juan Negrín’s government and exposed how ideological fractures can be as dangerous as external enemies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: An internal civil-war uprising in Barcelona resonates with an episode centered on music as protest and on how political rage spills onto the streets and stages.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1940, German forces occupied Brussels during their rapid invasion of Belgium, part of the blitzkrieg that stunned Western Europe in just weeks. The city’s fall underscored how modern mechanized warfare could shatter neutral borders and redraw maps with terrifying speed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A dark wartime occupation set against an episode about storms, resilience, and the belief that bleak chapters eventually give way to clearer skies.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1953, Delta Air Lines Flight 318 crashed near Marshall, Texas, killing 19 people when poor weather and navigation errors brought the DC‑3 down. Each such tragedy quietly reshaped aviation, prompting incremental safety improvements that make modern flying far safer than early passengers could imagine.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A plane crash that led to safer skies aligns with a reflective episode about injury, learning from pain, and slow, incremental recovery.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1967, Egyptian president Gamal Abdel Nasser demanded the withdrawal of the UN Emergency Force from Egypt, dismantling a key buffer between Israeli and Arab forces. The move accelerated the slide toward the Six-Day War, showing how pulling out peacekeepers can quickly turn a tense stalemate into open conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A pivotal Middle Eastern conflict pairs with an episode about Afrobeat’s political edge, both exploring how power, decolonization, and rhythm collide in the late 20th century.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1969, the Soviet Venera 6 probe’s descent into Venus revealed not just a thick atmosphere but pressure so crushing that even titanium-strength engineering survived only briefly. Its sacrifice helped confirm that Venus, once imagined as a jungle world, is in fact an extreme greenhouse with superheated, crushing air.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: The surreal, hostile atmosphere of Venus resonates with an episode that starts mysterious and atmospheric, then travels like a probe across musical ‘worlds.’

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1977, Atari founder Nolan Bushnell opened the first Chuck E. Cheese’s Pizza Time Theatre in San Jose, California, blending arcade games, pizza, and animatronic music shows. It pioneered the idea of a ‘family entertainment center,’ turning video games from dim arcades into bright, kid-friendly rituals.

episode.title: Morning Drops #07 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

queue.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode revel in playful nostalgia—whether it’s animatronic rodents and arcade cabinets or early‑2000s guilty‑pleasure hits.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1980, the Maoist guerrilla group Shining Path attacked a polling station in Chuschi, Peru, by burning ballot boxes on the eve of national elections. That seemingly small act of sabotage is widely seen as the spark that ignited a bloody internal conflict that would last for years.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A Latin American insurgency beginning at a ballot box mirrors an episode about a Latin artist whose work is steeped in political resistance and social critique.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1980, Miami erupted in days of rioting after an all-white jury acquitted four police officers who had beaten Arthur McDuffie, a Black insurance salesman, to death following a traffic stop. The unrest exposed deep racial inequities and became a grim case study in how justice systems can ignite or defuse social explosions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: A racially charged riot sparked by injustice aligns with Kendrick Lamar’s searing critiques of race, identity, and systems that box people in.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1983, the U.S. Department of Energy declassified documents revealing that 4.2 million pounds of mercury had been released from a nuclear weapons plant in Oak Ridge, Tennessee—likely the world’s largest mercury pollution event. The disclosure only came after a small regional newspaper pressed relentlessly with Freedom of Information Act requests.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A hidden environmental catastrophe exposed by persistent journalists pairs with an episode about curiosity, persistence, and learning as a force for long-term change.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1984, Prince Charles famously denounced a proposed modernist extension to London’s National Gallery as a “monstrous carbuncle,” an insult that sent shockwaves through the architectural world. His remark helped galvanize a backlash against stark modernist design and put royal opinion squarely into a debate about how cities should look.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: A royal critique that reshaped central London’s skyline fits with a London‑set episode steeped in the city’s architecture of sound and culture.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1987, an Iraqi Mirage F1 fighter fired two Exocet missiles into the USS Stark in the Persian Gulf, killing 37 American sailors and nearly sinking the frigate. The attack, officially labeled a mistake, underscored how crowded, ambiguous warzones can turn deadly in seconds—even between countries not formally at war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A sudden missile strike on a warship is a grim kind of 'electric shock', echoed metaphorically by an episode built around high-voltage, guitar-driven jolts.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1995, San Diego resident Shawn Nelson stole an M60 Patton tank from a National Guard armory and drove it through city streets, crushing cars and fire hydrants before being shot by police. The surreal rampage—caught live on TV—showed how even heavily armored weapons can be vulnerable to a single determined individual on the inside.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: A tank rampaging down city streets contrasts violently with the safer, communal ‘armored’ space of a dancefloor where people reclaim public space with music instead of machinery.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1997, rebel forces loyal to Laurent-Désiré Kabila entered Kinshasa, toppling Mobutu and renaming Zaire the Democratic Republic of the Congo. The change marked not just a political handover but a symbolic reclaiming of identity, as a postcolonial name imposed by a dictator gave way to a new national narrative.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A Congolese revolution and a Tuareg-focused episode both spotlight African movements rooted in desert landscapes, exile, resistance, and the struggle to redefine identity.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 2007, trains crossed the Korean Demilitarized Zone for the first time since 1953, running test journeys between North and South Korea across the 38th parallel. For a few hours, rails long severed by war carried steel and human hope in both directions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A cautious, symbolic reconnection between two Koreas aligns with an episode about slowing down, breathing, and gently reconnecting paths that were once cut.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1980, the Shining Path’s attack in Chuschi marked one kind of insurgency; but on the same date in another year, Charles’s “carbuncle” speech and other events show how cultural battles can be fought with words instead of weapons. Across history, May 17 has repeatedly been a day when people tried to redraw lines—whether on maps, buildings, or ballots.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: Whether attacking ballots or buildings, these events ask who gets to redesign shared spaces—echoing an episode about deliberately choosing how we design our own listening environments.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1969, the Venera program’s twin missions (including Venera 6) helped prove that Venus’s clouds hide not oceans but toxic, superheated air—an early warning about runaway greenhouse effects. In a sense, they turned a romantic ‘morning star’ into a laboratory for understanding climate extremes closer to home.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: An interplanetary mission that reframes how we see a neighboring world pairs with a cosmic, exploratory episode that treats listening itself as a voyage to another planet.

queue.fact_text: On May 17, 1969’s Venera descent and many other events of this date, machines and humans have pushed up against limits—of atmosphere, authority, and imagination. Whether it’s a probe crushed in Venusian clouds, a tank loose in city streets, or trains edging across the DMZ, May 17 is a reminder that boundaries can be both fragile and fiercely enforced.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A day full of threshold-crossing events fits the raw, origin-story energy of Sedna’s very first broadcast—a station itself crossing from idea into orbit.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1969, the Soviet probe Venera 6 plunged into the hellish atmosphere of Venus, transmitting pressure, temperature, and composition data until it was crushed by the planet’s immense weight of air. Its brief life helped reveal that Venus’s surface broils under pressures over 90 times Earth’s and temperatures hot enough to melt lead.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A space-probe plunging into Venus fits the cosmic, perspective-shifting theme of an episode about redefining emptiness as creative space.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 2006, the retired aircraft carrier USS Oriskany was deliberately sunk in the Gulf of Mexico to create one of the world’s largest artificial reefs. The 275-meter ‘Mighty O’ transformed from a Cold War warship into a thriving underwater skyscraper for coral, fish, and divers.

episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
